narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection from law school stings, especially when you've set your sights on becoming a lawyer, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—specifically, a 42% chance of not making it, which tipped the scales against admission. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest thread pulling things toward struggle was your Year 3 decile of 6, which stood out as the biggest signal that the academic climb might get tougher as classes ramped up. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 4, painting an early picture of being in the middle of the pack rather than leading the charge. Your LSAT score of 37 added to that cautious outlook, suggesting the intense reasoning demands of law school and the bar might not align as smoothly as we'd hope. Even with your upper-middle family income, which the model saw as a modest hurdle rather than a launchpad, it didn't quite offset the rest. Your white background and undergraduate GPA of 3.1 nudged things further toward that tougher road, as did opting for part-time status, which often means juggling more outside pressures. The one bright spot was your gender as male, which the model linked slightly to better odds of success. It's this combination—starting mid-pack, not accelerating strongly later, and those supporting factors stacking up—that made the model worry your story might end with a bar failure, even if you're determined and capable in ways numbers can't capture. This isn't the final word on your potential; it's just one data-driven lens urging a closer look at bolstering those academic markers before diving in. What do you think about targeting a higher LSAT retake or full-time focus to rewrite this narrative? Hang in there—you've got time to shape a stronger chapter.
